summary: The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a work session focused on zoning discussions, particularly PUD (Planned Unit Development) lot sizes, setbacks, and minimum house square footage requirements. Trustees and the zoning official worked through mathematical scenarios for lot dimensions, setbacks, and building footprints, ultimately discussing increasing the minimum PUD lot size from 5,000 to 6,600 square feet (60x110). They also prioritized upcoming zoning topics including accessory structures in front yards, the zone change process, and other hot-button issues for future meetings.
decisions: Motion to adjourn at 7:47 PM passed unanimously
action_items: Harry to prepare presentations on lot sizes, setbacks, and house size scenarios for different lot configurations
Harry to provide his top five hottest zoning complaint topics via email to help prioritize future discussions
Accessory structures in the front yard to be the primary topic at the next trustees meeting
Zone change process to also be discussed at the next meeting
Harry to find two more priority topics beyond accessory structures and zone change process for next meeting
Research needed on allowing accessory structures on vacant lots without a principal structure
Harry to send out previously prepared language on temporary structures for trustee review
Determine whether to bundle temporary structure language with other zoning changes or process separately
key_amounts: 5,000 sq ft - current minimum PUD lot size considered too small
6,600 sq ft (60x110) - proposed new minimum PUD lot size
1,400 sq ft - current minimum house square footage requirement
1,600 sq ft - maximum house footprint on a 6,600 sq ft lot with standard setbacks
400 sq ft - approximate two-car garage footprint (20x20)
35 ft - front and rear yard setbacks for township roads
50 ft - suggested minimum rear yard setback discussed by trustees
1,800 sq ft - building footprint needed on a 60x120 lot including garage
notable_topics: PUD lot size minimum increase from 5,000 to 6,600 square feet
Building setback requirements and their impact on lot size and house size
Ranch vs. two-story home feasibility on smaller lots
Accessory structures on vacant lots without principal structures
Zone change process improvements to speed up approvals
Temporary structures during new home construction
Future topics: tiny homes, Airbnb, food trucks, solar farms, corner lots, business in residential zones
notes: Meeting began with technical difficulties with the recording system. This appears to be part 1 of 2 of the meeting recording. Discussion referenced previous interactions with developers Ryan and Fisher regarding market studies. A previous tornado event was referenced as the impetus for temporary structure zoning language. Previously prepared temporary structure language from a former zoning law director has not yet been recorded/filed. The trustees appear to be conducting an extensive zoning code review that they expect to continue throughout the year.
title: BOT 1.31.22 1 of 2.mp4
